summary:
The Battle of Kadesh, fought in 1274 BC near the city of Kadesh on the Orontes River in modern Syria, was one of the largest chariot battles in ancient history and a pivotal confrontation between the two dominant powers of the Late Bronze Age: [[event:1807|Ramesses II]] of Egypt and Muwatalli II of the [[event:54|Hittite Empire]]. With an estimated 5,000–6,000 chariots combined, the engagement represented a clash of the period's supreme military technologies.

The battle began with an Egyptian intelligence failure. Ramesses II, advancing with his army divided into separate divisions, was fed false information by Hittite spies suggesting that the Hittite army was far away. In fact, a large Hittite chariot force was concealed behind the city of Kadesh. The Hittites ambushed the Egyptian Ra division and encircled the camp of Ramesses II himself. Egyptian accounts describe the pharaoh rallying his bodyguard and personally charging the Hittite chariots until relief arrived from another Egyptian division. The engagement ended inconclusively, with both sides withdrawing.

Both Ramesses II and the Hittites declared victory. The Egyptian record, preserved in the Poem of Pentaur and carved in elaborate reliefs on multiple temples, portrays Ramesses as a divine warrior who single-handedly turned the tide. Hittite records also claim the advantage. The strategic result was a stalemate that eventually led to the [[event:1809|Egyptian-Hittite Peace Treaty]] of 1259 BC — the earliest surviving peace treaty — under which both sides agreed to respect existing boundaries, exchange political refugees, and provide military assistance to each other against third parties.
